sentences.text: I'm Walter Austerer.
W-A-L-T-E-R A-U-S-T-E-R-E-R.
No.
November 27, 1923.
72 and 11 months.
In Vienna, Austria.
V-I-E-N-N-A.
Austria, A-U-S-T-R-I-A.
Moses and Rose.
My father was called Max, but his real name was Moses.
The same as mine.
Greenfield.
Well, I-- we lived in a Jewish neighborhood, the second district in Vienna.
And my grandmother lived about four blocks away from us.
And there were 11 children in her family, and she had 11 children.
Actually, there were two wives.
But there were 11, 11 children.
Two were killed in World War I, and the others were alive when I was a young child.
And most of them lived fairly close to us.
So there was a constant visiting.
And mostly, to my grandmother, but also between uncles, and aunts, and what have you.
And I had quite a few cousins.
So it was a fairly lively family life, although I was an only child.
That was my mother's mother.
And her husband died about a year before I was born, so I never knew him.
I never knew my grandfather.
And my father's family, he was born in Romania.
His mother died when he was two or three years old.
I don't know when his father died.
He had one brother in Chernivitsi, Romania.
And three brothers had emigrated to-- from Romania to the United States.
I have to think now.
I think Hilda, yeah.
No, it was an apartment.
It was a small apartment.
It was a one-bedroom apartment.
And I slept, actually, in the-- there was a large living room.
I slept in the corner.
It was partitioned off where I slept.
And my parents had the bedroom.
And there was a kitchen and the living room.
On the third floor.
A walk-up.
German.
Well, I-- I always like to construct things.
And I always had fun doing that.
And I also played a lot of soccer.
And then, later on, I-- I-- one of my cousins who was a violin teacher tried to teach me the violin, and not too successfully.
But I think it did instill a love of music for me.
And so it was really worthwhile.
I went to a public school from six to 10, four grades.
And then, I went to gymnasium, which was a-- sort of, a prep school for-- prepared you for college education.
In contrast to the-- what they called the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the folk school-- where children went to the age of 14.
And then after, if you wanted to enter a trade, you would then apprentice to a tailor, a shoemaker, whatever.
But if you decided to get a-- have a profession, you, you went to the other one, where you had to pay.
This was not a free school.
[NON-ENGLISH] .
And the gymnasium, was also-- which was close by, was also called [NON-ENGLISH] gymnasium.
Oh, about five blocks.
Well, I had a few friends.
My closest friend before leaving Vienna was Ziggy Diamond.
And he-- we were very close at that time.
I-- he left Vienna before I did.
I believe he went to Palestine.
I had never heard from him again.
I have no idea what, what happened to him.
And then, I had other friends.
I had a lot of friends in school.
And--
Primarily Jewish, because the, the school must have been about 85%, 80% Jewish because it was a Jewish district.
So--
Well, it-- there were non-Jews in our building, and it was very comfortable one.
There was no-- as far as I remember, there was no animosity.
The one, the one incident that I'll always remember was, we had a gym teacher that was in gymnasium.
And I, I didn't know that he was a Nazi.
He, he was.
And as soon as-- when-- just before the Nazis actually came in legally, he already wore a swastika button, as a lot of others did.
And-- nut he took us on an outing, a day's outing, and he got lost.
We went into the Alps, into the forerunners of the Alps, and he got lost.
And then, of course, for him that was a very unpleasant experience.
After all, he considered himself a very proper person and a knowledgeable person, and so on and so forth.
So-- and we had to do a lot of hiking.
We had-- in order to get back, we had probably hiked twice as much as we should have.
And it was pretty strenuous.

And so at the end of the day, he remarked, well, even in this school, I can see that there are-- that there are people who can take that kind of strenuous exercise, meaning that the Jews can also-- [LAUGHS] --manage to hike those distances.
And I always remember that because it was really the first time that a teacher came out with that kind of a remark.
And--
I was 14.
Well, it wasn't-- as I said, we were isolated in that school, really.

I'm, I'm sure pupils have been in-- children who had been in different districts, which were, you know, maybe, mostly Gentile or had a different mixture of Jews and Gentile would have heard that many times before.
But for us, as I said, you know, we were really isolated in that respect.
And on the other hand, anti-Semitism was all around us.
It was in the newspapers.
It was written about.
It was talked about.
So it was not a unique experience.
But in this school, it was.
As I said, I, I don't really-- again, because I lived in the Jewish district.
I, myself, didn't experience it until Hitler came in, until they actually marched in.
But also because-- probably because I was blond.
I had blond hair.
I had blue eyes.
I didn't look Jewish.
So I could get away with an awful lot.
And I'm sure that, that also prevented me from, from encountering it before.
Well, I think we should say before Hitler marched in.
And Hitler marched in in 1938.
Not before, no.
No, not that I experienced.
No.
Well, my father was in the textile business til about 1932, maybe.
And then, the depression, which actually-- worldwide depression-- which actually started in Austria because of the Creditanstalt Bank, which went bust, and then created the domino theory.
So things were terrible.
The unemployment rate was 20% or 25%.
And he-- his firm went out of business.
He lost his job.
And things went very, very bad, economically, for us for a couple of years.
Very bad.
He tried to, to sell on his own.
It didn't work out.
And finally then, maybe in 1933 or 1934, he went into the restaurant supply business and began to build up that business again.
And it was beginning to be fairly successful when he finally had to stop.
Not what-- for awhile, we observed the Sabbath.
And we did attend the services, the high holy days, and also, occasionally, during the year.
But not consistently, no.
Yes.
We-- I went-- there was a temple called the Polish Temple across the street from our building, which was burned down during Kristallnacht.
And now there's a plaque in-- on the building that they elected there.
The Polish Temple.
It was called the Polish Temple.
It was on Leopoldstrasse, right across the street from us.
And as a matter of fact, we could watch the, the choir rehearse and-- because it was that close.
We could look into that window where they rehearsed.
And-- but I was not bar mitvahed there That was a unorthodox one.
And I was bar mitvahed within a conservative one.
And I don't recall the name now.
A Very large synagogue in the-- near, near Praterstrasse.
But I don't remember the name of it.
Well, in school, religion was a subject that was taught in school.
So two or three times a week-- I forgot now, maybe twice a week-- we attended a class in religion.
And then I also attended, for about a couple of years, I attended, in addition, which was optional, in the afternoon, a Hebrew class, modern Hebrew class.
So I did that.
You mean general?
No, no.
The, the religious topics.
Oh, the religious topics.
The Bible, mostly.
That was taught in the, in the religious school, in the religious class.
And as I said, the optional taught modern Hebrew.
I-- that, again, was done by that Hebrew teacher who taught us in school, and-- the religious teacher.
Yes.
Well, I had to perform.
And then, afterwards, we had a party in my apartment.
And in those days, you got books and fountain pens, mostly.
[LAUGHS] There were about 12 kids.
My mother did the cooking.
And uncles and aunts came.
And that was the, the party.
And I think it was more meaningful then what is being done today.
That was in November.
Yeah.
I don't remember exactly anymore now.
No, no.
Well, I went with my father always.
As a matter of fact, on the high holy days, he went to another shul, which was close to my grandmother's house on [NON-ENGLISH], which isn't there anymore.
They've also been destroyed.
And I remember once, I was quite young.
And I went out into-- there was also a courtyard with chestnut trees.
And during the service, another friend of mine and myself-- well, maybe he wasn't a friend.
Maybe it was just kids, other kids there.
And we trying to knock down the chestnuts in the, the, that yard from trees.
And my father was looking for me.
And he came out.
And he was furious, of course.
And I felt terribly guilty.
[LAUGHS]
I remember that.
And how about Yom Kippur?
Did you-- do you have any memories of--
Well, that was on Yom Kippur.
Yeah.
It took place in Yom Kippur.
And then, we always went to my grandmother for-- afterwards, for the breakfast.
And was it just your family or all your cousins came and--
Quite a few of the cousins, yeah.
Not all, but quite a few of the children and grandchildren were there.
No, no.
It was much less.
Just, just-- I forgot what she prepared, actually.
I do remember that, the children-- she had two huge cans in her living room.
Like, milk cans.
Maybe they were milk cans.
And one was filled with plum jam, and the other one was filled with goose fat.
And when the children came, this is what you got.
You Had your choice.
So either taking slice of bread and, and putting that goose fat on it or the plum jam.
We usually made Passover.
Yeah.
We had it in our apartment.
And I remember a couple of years, my father invited destitute people.
I mean, we'd almost call them homeless people.
And they actually invited them to the seder.
I still remember that.
And I was very impressed by that.
Not really.
Not really.
No.
Birthday was always celebrated.
And I was always looking forward to it.
And I was always hoping to get another addition to my erector set.
And sometimes I did, and sometimes I didn't.
When things were terrible, I didn't.
But I always got a present.
Yes, Hanukkah I remember, too.
I remember, actually-- with my mother, my father traveled a lot when he was in the textile business.
But in the making-- what would we have made?
It was lead.
And we formed-- maybe we formed dreidels, and I think we did.
I forgot now how we did it, but I do remember doing it.
Yeah.
And celebrating Hanukkah.
That I remember.
A little bit, yeah.
Yeah, a couple of the, you know, [INAUDIBLE].
In Hebrew.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No.
I do remember, they did have-- we had-- the apartment house had a huge-- there were four courtyards, four apartment, apartment buildings who all had their courtyards.
And then, formed a square of courtyards.
And they would have been-- in one of them, they always had a succah.
But we didn't go there.
There  was another building.
And we didn't go there.
Well, I guess the people who lived in that particular building.
Well, in the-- in that Jewish district, it would've been fairly common, where there was an opportunity, where there was a courtyard.
Not every building had that kind of a courtyard.
And-- yeah.
That was a very big thing for me.
And as a matter of fact, I-- because I loved to build.
And as a matter of fact, when my young son was about eight years-- oh, maybe, eight-- I don't remember.
Seven or eight.
And I found out that Vienna still had those sets.
They were still making them.
And I wrote to a cousin of mine who lives there now and went back after the war.
And I told her to buy it for me.
And of course, I bought the largest one that existed.
You know, my, my dream.
And so I remember I was very excited and hoping that my younger son would be as interested in it as I was.
And he used it a little bit.
But then I, eventually, when he was sick, I built him an enormous carousel.
I think that was about the last either one of us did with it, but my wish had been fulfilled.
Well, not very much because-- I did go to the theater a few times, and that I loved.
Actually, I don't remember going to a concert.
And chances are, I wouldn't have it at that age.
But also because, as I said before, at age 10, 11, my father-- the depression was at its worst, and we really couldn't afford to, to buy tickets.
That was the last thing in a world that, that-- enough difficulty to just put food on the table.
And then, of course, year later, Hitler-- a couple years later, Hitler came in.
So there wasn't much of a chance.
But I did go to the theater.
And I still remember my first play, which was which I enjoyed enormously.
And then, I saw a couple of others.
Just a few.
Books, again, when you think about that, books were not readily available.
And I loved books.
And there was a, a private library.
I don't remember where there were any public ones.
I don't believe there were, except maybe the university library.
But there were little private libraries.
And I used those all the time.
And there was one-- there were two books that would come out.
Like, an encyclopedia came out every year.
One was scientific, and the other one was just travel and general.
And I was always looking forward to the next issue.
I couldn't wait to, to get those.
And sometimes I would-- a couple of times, I think, my father bought them for me.
Then, I remember when he was-- he had promised me that he would buy it for me, and, and-- the next one-- because I had done extremely well in school.
I was on top of tying for second place, I think.
And then, he lost his job.
And, and, you know, things were terrible.
And I never got that book, and I was terribly disappointed.
But I loved books and always read a lot of books, yeah.
We read all of that.
And we had-- I had an excellent education in gymnasium.
It was a marvelous foundation.
I had almost five years of Latin and all the German writers.
[NON-ENGLISH] And we read all of those, and the Greek mythology.
Yeah.
It was an amazing education for, for-- when you think about it, for a 11-year-old, 10-year-old, 11.
I was even younger because I still got into that class.
My birthday was late November, but I-- so I was younger than a lot of the kids in my class and almost a year ahead.

And to have that education, which, which was wonderful and I-- was such a big help when I came to this country because it was a foundation that had been laid, which allowed you to, to learn a lot afterwards, and no matter in what language or what.
French, a little bit of French.
We were beginning to learn French.
And then, of course, Hebrew, which I, you know, knew fairly well.
You mentioned earlier that one of your teachers was a Nazi, was wearing a swastika.
Mhm.
Yes.
For Instance, the doctor.
There was a doctor in my grandmother's house who we knew was a Nazi, even when it was illegal to be a Nazi.
He, after the war, committed suicide, from what I  had heard.
And there were other people, yes, whom you knew who were Nazis.
Austria was social democracy at that time.
Until about '37, maybe. '36 or '37.
And then, fascist-- sort of a semo-fascist government came in.
But yes, there were a lot of Nazis around.
